title: Mirabelle tart
subtitle: A French summer tart with golden plums set in almond cream
excerpt: Mirabelle plums are small, honey-sweet stone fruit with less acidity than red plums. Here they are halved and pressed cut-side up into frangipane in a blind-baked sweet pastry case, where they caramelise and soften during the second bake. A final brush of apricot glaze gives a professional finish. The total time, including resting the pastry, is about 2 hours.
sourceNotes: Mirabelle plums are particularly associated with Lorraine in north-eastern France, which hosts an annual mirabelle festival and produces the majority of Europe's commercial crop. The fruit appears in tarts and preserves in Escoffier and later in regional French cookbooks. The frangipane-and-stone-fruit tart format is documented across French patisserie, with the Bourdaloue tart (pear frangipane) the most famous variant. Richard Bertinet gives a standard method in 'Pastry' (2012).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The blind-baked pastry case keeps at room temperature for up to 24 hours. The frangipane can be refrigerated for up to 2 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; blind-bake at 190 degrees C, then reduce to 180 degrees C for the filled tart

glossaryTerms.definition: Small, round stone fruit that ripen to a golden-yellow colour with a honey-sweet flavour and less acidity than most red plums. They grow mainly in Lorraine, France, and are in season from late July to mid-August. Mirabelles hold their shape during baking better than soft plums, and their natural sweetness means the pastry base needs less added sugar. Greengages or small Victoria plums are the closest substitute; avoid large, acidic plums which will overwhelm the frangipane.

content.text: are best here; they hold their shape through both bakes and their sweetness needs no adjustment in this recipe. Tinned mirabelles work if fresh are unavailable: drain them well and pat dry before using.

Blind-bake the pastry case completely before adding the frangipane. The base must be dry and pale golden before the filling goes in; an underbaked base will not crisp beneath the weight of the almond filling.

Rub the 100 g cold butter into the 200 g flour and 40 g icing sugar until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Mix in the egg yolk and enough ice-cold water to bring together into a dough. Flatten into a disc, wrap and chill for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Roll the pastry out and line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin. Prick the base, line with baking paper and fill with baking beans. Blind-bake for 15 minutes, then remove the paper and beans and bake for a further 5 to 8 minutes until the base is pale golden and dry. Reduce the oven to 180 degrees C.

Beat the 100 g softened butter and 100 g caster sugar until pale. Beat in the 2 eggs one at a time. Fold in the 100 g ground almonds, 15 g flour and 0.5 tsp almond extract. Spread into the blind-baked case. Arrange the 350 g mirabelle halves cut-side up over the frangipane, pressing them in gently. Bake at 180 degrees C fan for 35 to 40 minutes until the frangipane is puffed, deep golden brown, and set with only the faintest wobble at the very centre. Cool in the tin.
While the tart is still warm, brush the surface with the warmed, sieved apricot jam. This sets as it cools, giving a professional gloss. Remove from the tin when completely cool.

items.solution: The blind bake was shortened or skipped. The base must be fully dry and pale golden before the frangipane goes in; if you can press the base with a finger and feel it is still slightly soft, return it to the oven without the beans for a further 5 minutes.

items.problem: Frangipane is still liquid after 40 minutes
items.solution: The fruit released too much juice. Mirabelles should be patted dry before placing in the frangipane. If the centre is still wet, cover the tart loosely with foil and continue baking at 170 degrees C for a further 10 minutes.
The frangipane was too soft. Make sure the butter for the frangipane is softened but not melted, and that the eggs are at room temperature. A firmer frangipane will hold the fruit near the surface.
